name: Andrew Bragg
background:
Senator Bragg is a member of the Moderate faction of the Liberal Party with a focus on financial regulation, housing, and Indigenous reconciliation. His professional background in accounting and executive policy roles—specifically at the Financial Services Council and the Business Council of Australia—has shaped his ongoing scrutiny of the superannuation sector and his push for industry reform. He is a strong advocate for parliamentary supremacy and has championed market-oriented solutions, including the regulation of cryptocurrency and the implementation of the Consumer Data Right.

His parliamentary work is defined by extensive committee experience, including leadership in inquiries into corporate regulation (ASIC), housing affordability, and residential electrification. Beyond economic policy, Bragg is a notable supporter of the 2017 Uluru Statement from the Heart and has authored works on Indigenous rights. His advocacy style is characterised by a willingness to challenge institutional norms, as evidenced by his efforts to increase disclosure requirements for industry superannuation funds and his public commitment to net-zero carbon policies, which he has stated is a condition of his continued frontbench service.

name: Andrew McLachlan
background:
Senator McLachlan is a member of the Liberal Party’s centrist faction, having previously aligned with the Moderate/Modern Liberal grouping. His political approach is characterised by a strong commitment to the rule of law and the principle of the separation of powers. This dedication to institutional integrity was evidenced in his 2015 floor-crossing against party lines on "anti-bikie" legislation, which he argued improperly conflated parliamentary and judicial functions. He has also demonstrated interest in civil liberties and press freedom, notably through his unsuccessful attempt to introduce shield laws to protect journalists’ sources.

Professionally, McLachlan brings extensive experience in law, financial services, and military service to his role. A qualified lawyer specialising in banking, finance, and superannuation, he has held senior executive roles and board positions within the financial services sector. His military background as a Colonel in the Australian Army Legal Corps, for which he received a Conspicuous Service Cross, and his personal commitment to commemorating veterans, highlight a pragmatic and principled approach to his legislative duties. On matters of foreign policy, he has navigated complex geopolitical issues by advocating for a balanced, negotiated two-state solution to the Israeli-Palestinian conflict.

name: Anne Ruston
background:
Senator Ruston is a member of the Liberal Party’s Moderate faction. Her political career has often balanced internal party dynamics, having been appointed to the Senate through a contested preselection process and a casual vacancy. While her voting record generally reflects her party alignment, she has demonstrated a willingness to take socially conservative positions, notably voting in favour of a motion to ban sex-selective abortion in 2017.

Her formative professional background is rooted in business and the agricultural sector rather than traditional advocacy or unionism. Before entering the Senate, she served as the inaugural CEO of the National Wine Centre and managed a commercial rose-growing business in Renmark. Her experience in private industry, particularly in navigating economic challenges like the Millennium drought and project management within the wine and tourism sectors, informs her policy focus on agriculture, water resources, and economic administration. Her ministerial tenure—which included portfolios for Families and Social Services, women’s safety, and international development—suggests an interest in the intersection of social welfare delivery and fiscal management.

name: Anthony Chisholm
background:
Senator Chisholm is a member of Labor’s right faction and possesses a deep background in party machinery, having served as Queensland state secretary. His formative career includes grassroots organising for Labor and working as an advisor to Kevin Rudd, transitioning later into the corporate sector at Santos, where he gained experience in navigating the intersection of industry interests and stakeholder advocacy.

His parliamentary career reflects a commitment to regional development and national oversight, evidenced by his ministerial portfolios in regional development, agriculture, fisheries, forestry, and resources. Key advocacy highlights include his role as deputy chair of the inquiry into the destruction of the Juukan Gorge caves, demonstrating a focus on heritage protection, and his leadership of the Select Committee on the Administration of Sports Grants. His policy focus is heavily influenced by his Queensland roots and a long-standing career engagement with both political strategy and industrial policy.

name: Barbara Pocock
background:
Senator Pocock’s political identity is rooted in a career-long commitment to industrial relations, labour rights, and gender equity. An economist and former academic, she has dedicated her professional life to critiquing wealth inequality and the "work-life collision," consistently advocating for the empowerment of trade unions and the protection of casual workers. Her ideological focus is firmly aligned with social justice, as evidenced by her past leadership at the Australia Institute and her pioneering research into the systemic challenges faced by low-paid workers and working carers.

Her legislative approach is informed by significant professional experience, including a foundational period at the Reserve Bank of Australia and extensive academic work in vocational education. As a parliamentarian, she has leveraged this expertise to champion reforms in income support, the regulation of the consultancy sector—notably following the PwC tax scandal—and the removal of barriers for younger generations, such as student debt and housing insecurity. Her advocacy is further shaped by her background in regional South Australia and her long-term engagement with social movements, including environmental campaigns against nuclear waste and ongoing support for LGBTQ+ and refugee rights.

name: Carol Brown
background:
Senator Brown is a member of the Labor Left faction with a deep-seated commitment to the party’s values, tracing her involvement to a strong family history of union and Labor activism in Tasmania. Her formative professional background is firmly rooted in public service and party administration, having served as an administrative officer for the Labor Party, a political adviser to Senator Sue Mackay, and in various portfolio management roles within the Tasmanian state government, including infrastructure, energy, and environmental sectors.

Her policy career has been defined by her tenure as the Shadow Minister for Disability and Carers, alongside her recent executive experience as Assistant Minister for Infrastructure and Transport. Following her transition from the ministry in 2024, she has moved into a significant legislative oversight role as the chair of the Joint Standing Committee on Electoral Matters. Her long-standing engagement with Tasmania’s political landscape is bolstered by multi-generational family ties to the party, positioning her as a parliamentarian with a granular understanding of both local administrative processes and the federal Labor legislative agenda.

name: Charlotte Walker
background:
Senator Walker aligns with the Labor Left faction, with her political advocacy deeply rooted in addressing systemic barriers for young Australians. Her worldview is shaped by her professional background with the Australian Services Union and her grassroots involvement as president of Young Labor in South Australia. These experiences, combined with her unconventional, digitally native campaign style, suggest a commitment to progressive outreach and a preference for direct, relatable communication on complex policy issues.

Her policy agenda is defined by a strong focus on cost-of-living relief, housing affordability, and the reduction of student HECS debts. Her parliamentary contributions highlight a dedication to mental health reform, domestic violence prevention, and environmental protection, specifically regarding South Australian algal blooms. Her advocacy is further informed by personal lived experiences with bullying, depression, and digital exploitation, the latter of which has directly influenced her support for a social media ban for those under 16.

name: Claire Chandler
background:
Senator Chandler is a member of the Liberal Party’s National Right faction and holds clear conservative views. Her political outlook is defined by a strong emphasis on traditional, sex-based definitions of rights, leading her to campaign extensively against the expansion of transgender rights in sports and public facilities. She is also a vocal critic of what she terms "the woke," positioning herself as a defender of free speech against institutional or taxpayer-funded suppression. While she maintains a preference for conscience votes on sensitive moral issues like euthanasia, her legislative record reflects a focus on protecting single-sex spaces, exemplified by her introduction of the "Save Women's Sport" Bill in 2022.

Her worldview is shaped by a professional background in risk advisory and corporate analysis at Deloitte, alongside deep roots in the Liberal Party’s internal structures, having served as both the Tasmanian Division and Federal President of the Young Liberals. Her earlier work involved investigating gender imbalances within the party, though she notably rejected gender quotas as a policy solution. These formative experiences—transitioning from corporate risk assessment to internal party leadership and advocacy—underpin her approach to policy, which balances a pragmatic, results-oriented focus with firm ideological principles regarding traditional social structures and individual expression.

name: Corinne Mulholland
background:
Senator Mulholland is aligned with the Labor Right faction, a positioning that suggests a pragmatic approach to policy-making within the Labor party structure. Her professional background is defined by 15 years of experience across local and state government, complemented by her time as a lobbyist for Star Casino. This extensive career in both public administration and the corporate sector indicates an understanding of the intersection between government processes and commercial interests.

Her political trajectory includes a previous unsuccessful candidacy for the Division of Petrie in 2019, demonstrating long-term persistence in her parliamentary ambitions. Additionally, her previous service as a board member for 'The Common Good' suggests an engagement with the non-profit or charitable sector, providing a potential avenue for connecting her professional history to social welfare advocacy. There is currently no public information available regarding her specific parliamentary committee memberships or stated policy platforms.

name: Dave Sharma
background:
A member of the Liberal Party’s Moderate/Modern Liberal faction, Sharma approaches policy through a "realist" lens, prioritising international diplomacy, military investment, and national security. His worldview is heavily informed by a career as a public servant and diplomat, including his tenure as head of the International Division of the Department of the Prime Minister and Cabinet and as Ambassador to Israel. These roles have fostered a focus on statecraft, the importance of global alliances, and a pragmatic, often bipartisan, approach to foreign policy and human rights, evidenced by his advocacy for the extradition of Malka Leifer.

Domestically, Sharma has positioned himself as a progressive voice within his party on specific social and environmental issues. He has championed net-zero emissions targets, supported same-sex marriage, and crossed the floor to protect transgender students, often distancing himself from the party's more conservative elements. While he advocates for climate action, his stance is tempered by an opposition to what he describes as "totalitarian" electric vehicle mandates. His parliamentary career is marked by a willingness to challenge party leadership on select moral and environmental concerns, reflecting a desire to align the Liberal Party with more centrist, urban-professional values.

name: David Pocock
background:
Pocock’s political identity is defined by social progressivism, a strong emphasis on government integrity, and evidence-based policy. His legislative agenda focuses on climate action, increasing renewable energy expenditure, and restoring territory rights, particularly regarding euthanasia. He is a pragmatic negotiator, demonstrated by his successful effort to establish the Economic Inclusion Advisory Committee to provide independent advice on welfare adequacy in exchange for supporting industrial relations reforms. While registered as a party for electoral purposes, he maintains a strict independent posture in the Senate, prioritising cross-bench influence over major-party alignment.

His worldview is heavily informed by his background as a high-profile activist and conservationist. His early life in Zimbabwe, marked by the compulsory acquisition of his family's farm, and his later graduate studies in sustainable agriculture have shaped his focus on environmental protection and community resilience. His advocacy career includes long-standing public campaigns for marriage equality and direct action against coal mining expansion. His personal and political approach frequently highlights a commitment to accessibility and inclusion, as evidenced by his pursuit of formal recognition for Auslan interpreters within the Senate.

name: David Shoebridge
background:
Shoebridge’s political trajectory is defined by a transition from a corporate legal background to a fervent advocate for civil liberties, worker protections, and government transparency. Having initially been involved with Labor, he moved to the Greens, where he has been associated with more radical, anti-capitalist perspectives, including support for the Left Renewal faction. His worldview is heavily informed by his professional history as a barrister specialising in employment and discrimination law, as well as a formative period in his youth involving experiences with domestic abuse, which has underscored his long-standing commitment to protecting vulnerable populations, including survivors of child sexual abuse.

His parliamentary career is characterised by "ferocious" opposition tactics, high-profile legislative inquiries, and persistent advocacy against police overreach, exemplified by his "Sniff Off" campaign. He has demonstrated a willingness to work across the aisle to secure government accountability, notably in matters involving public grant distribution and cultural institutions. His current federal portfolio interests—Justice, Defence, and Digital Rights—align with his history of campaigning against state power, opposing the organ trade, and advocating for human rights, including vocal support for Palestinian causes and the movement to end the extradition of Julian Assange.

name: Dean Smith
background:
Senator Smith is a factionally unaligned member of the Liberal Party with a background in political strategy, having served as a policy adviser to former WA Premier Richard Court and as a senior adviser to Prime Minister John Howard. His professional history also includes founding a lobbying firm and serving as treasurer of the WA Liberal state branch. He describes himself as a "constitutional revolutionary" and maintains membership in organisations such as Australians for Constitutional Monarchy and the Australian National Flag Association.

His parliamentary career is defined by his role as the first openly-LGBTI member of the federal Liberal Party and his sponsorship of the 2017 marriage equality legislation, a shift from his previously stated opposition to the issue. He is an Anglican who was the first in his family to attend university. Regarding policy, his committee work has included the inquiry into the destruction of Juukan Gorge; in this capacity, he co-authored dissenting comments opposing new federal cultural heritage legislation, prioritising the concerns of the mining industry over additional Commonwealth oversight.

name: Jacqui Lambie
background:
Senator Lambie’s political identity is rooted in a populist, grassroots approach that prioritises veteran welfare, economic sovereignty, and the struggles of low-income Australians. Initially associated with the Palmer United Party, she established the Jacqui Lambie Network (JLN) to maintain ideological independence, often shifting positions based on pragmatism and evolving personal experiences—such as her pivot toward a pro-refugee stance following direct engagement with war-affected populations. She frequently frames her advocacy around themes of "mateship, respect, and integrity," championing causes like stricter political donation transparency, support for domestic manufacturing, and opposition to higher education reforms that might disadvantage students from lower socioeconomic backgrounds.

Her worldview is profoundly shaped by her military service in the Australian Army and subsequent, lengthy conflict with the Department of Veterans' Affairs regarding medical compensation and support for mental health. This personal experience with institutional failure was a key catalyst for her successful campaign to establish a Royal Commission into Defence and Veteran Suicide. Furthermore, her background—having been raised in public housing and experiencing personal and familial battles with addiction—informs her vocal focus on youth unemployment, mental health, and the need for greater support for vulnerable Australians, often positioning herself against perceived "boys' club" political cultures.

name: James Paterson
background:
Senator Paterson is a prominent figure within the national right faction of the Liberal Party, holding a philosophical commitment to libertarian principles, including free-market economics and robust protections for freedom of speech. His formative professional background is rooted in the Institute of Public Affairs (IPA), where he served in senior roles including deputy executive director, a period that heavily influenced his advocacy for reduced government intervention and economic reform.

His parliamentary career has been defined by his work on national security and foreign affairs, most notably as the former Chair of the Parliamentary Joint Committee on Intelligence and Security. He is a vocal critic of the Chinese government regarding human rights and foreign interference, while simultaneously championing civil liberty issues such as the proposed reform of section 18C of the Racial Vilification Act. Personally, while he identifies as agnostic, he navigates a family life that includes Catholic traditions, and he has previously sought to bridge conservative and liberal perspectives on social issues such as same-sex marriage through legislative alternatives.

name: Jana Stewart
background:
Senator Stewart is a member of the Labor Unity faction and maintains strong ties to the trade union movement through the Transport Workers’ Union. Her legislative focus is deeply informed by her professional background as a clinical family therapist, university lecturer, and policy adviser. As the current chair of the Joint Standing Committee on Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Affairs, she is uniquely positioned to lead on policy areas concerning Indigenous justice and child protection.

Her political perspective is heavily shaped by her personal history as a Muthi Muthi and Wamba-Wamba woman who overcame childhood adversity and systemic educational barriers. Having previously worked on Stolen Generations reparations and treaty negotiations for the Victorian government, she prioritises social justice, child welfare, and Indigenous rights. Her formative experiences as a mother and practitioner provide a pragmatic, service-oriented foundation for her advocacy on national policy.

name: Jess Walsh
background:
Senator Walsh is a staunchly pro-union, Labor Left-aligned figure whose political ideology is deeply rooted in the protection of low-wage workers and the reform of insecure service-sector employment. Her academic background—a PhD in economic geography focusing on labour and urban politics—complements a formative career spent as a long-term state secretary for United Voice. Her advocacy is driven by a focus on systemic economic fairness and the challenges posed by the erosion of stable, manufacturing-based employment in favour of precarious service work.

In her capacity as Minister for Early Childhood Education and Minister for Youth, and through her prior work chairing the Senate Economics Committee, Senator Walsh maintains a clear focus on the intersection of economic policy and social welfare. Her legislative approach is informed by years of grassroots organising and research at progressive think tanks, suggesting a preference for policy interventions that strengthen community and labour-based protections. Advocates should engage her by framing social causes within the context of economic equity, job stability, and the long-term societal benefits of investing in early childhood and youth support systems.

name: Jessica Collins
background:
Senator Collins is a central figure in a significant internal shift within the Liberal Party, having demonstrated a clear willingness to challenge established leadership early in her parliamentary career. Her pivotal role in the February 2026 leadership spill motion alongside Phillip Thompson signals a strategic, reform-oriented approach to party politics and a desire to influence the ideological direction of the Coalition from the outset of her term.

Her path to the Senate was marked by her position as a preferred candidate over established incumbents, suggesting she holds strong backing from key party powerbrokers. Beyond these factional activities, the provided record does not detail her pre-parliamentary career, specific committee memberships, or personal advocacy history, indicating that her public political identity is currently defined primarily by her recent decisive actions regarding party leadership and internal parliamentary manoeuvring.

name: Jonathon Duniam
background:
Senator Duniam is a member of the Liberal Party’s National Right faction. His political outlook is shaped by a career as a long-term political staffer, including roles advising senior conservative senators and serving as deputy chief of staff to former Tasmanian Premier Will Hodgman. His legislative priorities are heavily focused on regional industry and environmental regulation, notably advocating for the forestry and fishing sectors. He has been particularly active in opposing the funding of the Environmental Defenders Office and has positioned himself as a staunch defender of the salmon farming industry in Macquarie Harbour against federal intervention.

His personal values are influenced by his Christian faith—attending the Mount Stuart Presbyterian Church—and his deep roots as a sixth-generation Tasmanian. While he has historically held conservative views on social issues, he has demonstrated a capacity for pragmatic shifts, such as voting in favour of the marriage equality legislation to reflect public sentiment in his electorate. Furthermore, his familial background is notable for its intersection with both sides of politics; he is the grandson of a former Labor Legislative Council candidate, though he has remained firmly within the Liberal Party since joining in 2001.

name: Jordon Steele-John
background:
Steele-John’s political identity is defined by his commitment to disability rights, systemic reform, and progressive social change. His advocacy is rooted in a focus on accountability and human rights, evidenced by his successful campaign for the Royal Commission into Violence, Abuse, Neglect and Exploitation of People with Disability and his legislative efforts to protect confidential disclosures. He champions a platform of expanded public healthcare—specifically mental health and ADHD support—and advocates for structural shifts such as lowering the voting age, increasing the Disability Support Pension, and returning war-making powers from the executive branch to Parliament.

His worldview is heavily informed by his personal lived experience as a wheelchair user with cerebral palsy and his early immersion in grassroots activism. Having joined the Greens at sixteen and engaged in multiple federal and state election campaigns before entering the Senate, he views political participation through a lens of accessibility and inclusion. His legislative work reflects a consistent focus on the intersection of social welfare and civil rights, complemented by his interest in fostering emerging industries like Australian video game development and his ongoing opposition to environmental practices such as fracking.

name: Josh Dolega
background:
Senator Dolega’s ideological foundation is firmly rooted in the Labor Left. His political trajectory is defined by a significant background in the labour movement, most notably as the work health and safety lead organiser for the Community and Public Sector Union (CPSU). This experience, combined with a 15-year tenure at the Australian Taxation Office and work as a fitness instructor, suggests a career perspective deeply attuned to workplace protections, public service, and the practical challenges faced by everyday workers.

His lived experience as an openly gay man, combined with his roots in the Devonport region and professional history in Burnie and Hobart, informs his approach to representation. Having risen through the party ranks with the backing of the Australian Manufacturing Workers Union and his predecessor, Anne Urquhart, he is positioned as a voice for the north-west coast within the Senate. His advocacy profile is currently shaped by his mandate to represent Tasmanian interests and his commitment to the broader Labor platform as he serves the remainder of his predecessor’s term.

name: Karen Grogan
background:
Senator Grogan’s political identity is deeply rooted in the Progressive Left faction of the Labor party and the trade union movement, having served as a national political coordinator for the United Workers Union. Her ideological commitment to social and economic justice is heavily informed by her upbringing in a union-active household and her professional background in the social services sector, including her tenure as CEO of the South Australian Council of Social Service. Her career demonstrates a consistent focus on systemic advocacy, spanning education, mental health, the environment, Aboriginal issues, and scientific policy through her work with the Wentworth Group of Concerned Scientists.

Her legislative perspective is uniquely shaped by a period of significant personal hardship in the mid-1990s, where she experienced financial instability, illness, and the loss of employment, leading her to rely on the social safety net and the support of her local community. This lived experience with the welfare system serves as a foundational driver for her parliamentary advocacy. Having held high-level advisory roles for prominent Labor figures—including Mark Butler, Jay Weatherill, and Peter Malinauskas—she possesses a strategic understanding of executive government and organisational politics, which complements her current role as Government Deputy Whip in the Senate.

name: Katy Gallagher
background:
Senator Gallagher is a member of the Labor Left faction with a strong background in social work and advocacy. Her professional history as a community worker for the intellectually disabled and as a national organiser for the Community and Public Sector Union (CPSU) informs her approach to public policy, emphasising social support systems and the role of the union movement. Her political career is characterised by a focus on health, disability services, and industrial relations, as well as a commitment to expanding civil rights, most notably her oversight of the ACT’s same-sex marriage legislation and the successful repeal of federal bans on territories legislating on euthanasia.

Her legislative priorities have consistently centred on government transparency, public service accountability, and equity. As an advocate, she has scrutinised government grant programs and public expenditure, while her ministerial roles have seen her navigate significant portfolios including Finance, Women, and the Public Service. Personally, Gallagher’s perspective is shaped by her upbringing in Canberra and significant life challenges, including the loss of her fiancé in a 1997 accident. These experiences, combined with her experience chairing the parliamentary committee into the COVID-19 pandemic, demonstrate a focus on crisis management, evidence-based policy, and the importance of accessible public services for families and the community.

name: Kerrynne Liddle
background:
Senator Liddle’s political identity is shaped by her background in corporate management and Indigenous advocacy, having served on the boards of the Central Australian Aboriginal Congress, Indigenous Business Australia, and the South Australian Housing Trust. A member of the Liberal Party’s Centrist faction, her worldview is informed by a career spanning journalism, public relations, and executive roles at Santos Limited and Voyages Indigenous Tourism Australia. Her professional history demonstrates a focus on practical economic participation and institutional governance.

Having been born in Alice Springs to parents who were a construction worker and a nurse, she possesses deep roots in regional and Indigenous community service. Her career prior to politics reflects a consistent engagement with Indigenous media policy and professional development, underscored by her academic background in management and business. As a former member of the Labor Party for a brief period in the mid-2000s, her move to the Liberal Party and subsequent rise to the Senate highlight an ideological focus on pragmatism and individual empowerment over traditional partisan orthodoxy.

name: Larissa Waters
background:
Senator Waters’ political career is deeply rooted in her extensive background as an environmental lawyer, having spent nearly a decade with the Environmental Defenders Office prior to her election. Her core advocacy is defined by a strong focus on environmental protection, gender equity, and the systemic reform of political donations to reduce corporate influence. She has consistently championed efforts to end gender-based violence and has used her parliamentary position to advocate for significant policy shifts regarding international relations and military accountability, specifically regarding the sale of weapons components to Israel.

Her political identity is shaped by her experiences navigating both professional legal practice and the challenges of balancing motherhood with high-level public service, most notably marking a milestone for parliamentary representation by breastfeeding in the Senate chamber. While she has held senior leadership roles within the Greens—including co-deputy leader and her current position as leader of the party—her career has also been defined by periods of transition, including the 2017 constitutional eligibility crisis and her subsequent return to the Senate. These experiences highlight a persistent engagement with institutional processes and a career trajectory marked by high-profile advocacy and party leadership.

name: Lidia Thorpe
background:
Senator Thorpe’s political identity is defined by a steadfast commitment to the Blak Sovereign Movement and a rejection of colonial institutions, which she views as illegitimate. Her ideological positioning is rooted in advocating for treaty-first processes, truth-telling, and Indigenous sovereignty, leading her to oppose the 2023 Voice to Parliament proposal as "window dressing." Her parliamentary approach is characterised by disruptive, high-profile protest tactics aimed at challenging the status quo, and she maintains a cross-bench independence that allows her to align with diverse political actors on specific issues, such as her noted rapport with senators from the National Party and One Nation.

Her worldview is shaped by a career in community advocacy and frontline social service, having served as co-chair of the Victorian NAIDOC Committee, a manager at the Lake Tyers Aboriginal Training Centre, and in roles with Centrelink and the East Gippsland Shire Council. These experiences are informed by a multi-generational family legacy of activism, including her grandmother’s role in the Aboriginal Tent Embassy. Thorpe’s personal history—marked by navigating housing commission living, single parenthood, and overcoming domestic violence and bankruptcy—directly influences her focus on social justice, Indigenous rights, and her critique of the parliamentary environment as a "violent" and exclusionary workplace.

name: Lisa Darmanin
background:
Senator Darmanin’s worldview is deeply rooted in her long-term career as a trade unionist, beginning as an active workplace delegate at Berry Street Victoria in 1997. Her professional background reflects a strong commitment to industrial relations, human resource management, and community development. Her work as an organiser for the Australian Services Union (ASU) and her eventual leadership as Branch Secretary suggest a strategic focus on organised labour, social equity, and collective bargaining.

Her experience extends to public sector initiatives, having worked on gender equality and family violence prevention within the Victorian Department of Premier and Cabinet. Furthermore, her involvement in the "Your Rights at Work" campaign and her tenure as Chair of Vision Super demonstrate a policy interest in superannuation governance and the protection of workers' rights. As a resident of Melbourne with two sons, her professional trajectory strongly aligns with advocating for the interests of the community and social services sectors.

name: Malcolm Roberts
background:
Senator Roberts aligns with conservative and nationalist ideologies, exhibiting strong opposition to globalist institutions, particularly the United Nations and the Paris Agreement, which he views as threats to Australian sovereignty. His political rhetoric is heavily influenced by climate change denial, a belief in "anti-human" socialist conspiracies, and a suspicion of mainstream scientific and governmental bodies. He has actively campaigned for the repeal of section 18C of the Racial Discrimination Act and supports the dismantling of union influence through the re-establishment of the Australian Building and Construction Commission.

His worldview is largely shaped by his background as a mining engineer and manager in the coal industry, alongside his later transition into full-time political activism through the Galileo Movement. His professional history, which includes a focus on management consultancy and alternative self-help methodologies, informs his confrontational approach to bureaucracy and his preference for "empirical" evidence—though he is frequently at odds with established scientific consensus. While he has previously interacted with rhetoric associated with the sovereign citizen movement, he has formally rejected that identification, maintaining a political identity focused on dismantling perceived institutional corruption.

name: Maria Kovacic
background:
Senator Kovacic identifies as a "progressive liberal," and her factional standing is anchored within the Liberal Party's Moderate wing, bolstered by support from the Centre Right. Her worldview is informed by a 16-year career as an ANZ mobile lending franchisee and her tenure as a director of the Franchise Council of Australia (2020–2023). This background, combined with her active participation in Western Sydney community groups, suggests a practical, business-orientated approach to policy development.

Her personal history as the daughter of post-war migrants from the former Yugoslavia provides a context for her engagement with Australian social and economic life. Notable for a Liberal parliamentarian, she has expressed a willingness to challenge party orthodoxy on economic policy, specifically advocating for a cap on negative gearing for investment properties. Her trajectory reflects a transition from grassroots community advocacy and party organisational leadership to the federal sphere, prioritising moderate policy positions and regional economic concerns.

name: Marielle Smith
background:
Senator Smith is a member of the Labor Right faction with a strong background in public policy and ministerial advisory roles, having served as a researcher and adviser to Kate Ellis and a senior policy adviser to former Prime Minister Julia Gillard. Her formative career includes experience in the Australian Public Service and volunteer work in Ghana and Sierra Leone, complemented by a private sector background as a former board member of Transit Systems.

Her primary policy focus, articulated in her maiden speech, is the expansion of early childhood education, specifically advocating for universal funding for preschool for three-year-olds to ensure equitable access regardless of socio-economic background. Beyond her parliamentary duties, her personal context is defined by her education at the Australian National University and the London School of Economics, her ties to South Australia, and her experience balancing family life as a parent.

name: Matt Canavan
background:
Senator Canavan’s ideological framework is rooted in economic liberalism and strong regional conservatism. His professional background as an economist for the Productivity Commission and executive at KPMG, combined with his tenure as chief of staff to Barnaby Joyce, underscores an approach that prioritises primary industries, particularly mining and fossil fuels. He is a staunch critic of net zero targets and renewable energy investment, maintaining a firm pro-development stance for Northern Australia. His factional alignment remains firmly within the right-leaning wing of the Nationals, often positioning himself against progressive social movements, which he has characterised as "woke."

His political advocacy is defined by an opposition to social mandates and an emphasis on traditional values. He has consistently opposed same-sex marriage, abortion on gender grounds, and vaccine mandates, while frequently using his platform to challenge contemporary discourse on race and gender, including the criticism of diversity initiatives in media. His committee work includes an inquiry into the destruction of cultural heritage at Juukan Gorge, and his voting record reflects a consistent rejection of government social media restrictions and environmental climate action. His personal worldview is further informed by a return to his Roman Catholic faith and his upbringing in the regional and outer-suburban areas of Queensland.
